title: Whakairo Toiora: Carving the Living River / Te Awa Ora: He Whakairo i te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway for Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa holds stories of ancestral journeys, provides sustenance, and embodies the concept of mauri (life force). Students will learn how traditional carving patterns (kōwhaiwhai) tell the story of the awa's health and how contemporary artists are using these patterns to advocate for environmental protection.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret traditional Ngāti Kahungunu kōwhaiwhai patterns, identifying their connection to the local environment and narratives of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Design and create a contemporary whakairo panel or sculpture using reclaimed materials, incorporating kōwhaiwhai patterns that narrate a personal or collective response to the health of the awa.
Articulate, using appropriate arts terminology, how their artwork expresses the concept of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and reflects the mauri of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Collaboratively plan and contribute to a class exhibition that shares their artworks and their message of cultural and environmental preservation with the school community.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero: Walking the Story of the Awa
activities.description: A guided hīkoi (walk) along a restored section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, led by a kaumātua (elder) from the local marae. Students will sketch the natural forms (leaves, water flow, birdlife) and listen to pūrākau (stories) and waiata (songs) connected to the awa, gathering visual and narrative inspiration.

activities.title: Te Whakapapa o te Whakairo: Decoding the Patterns
activities.description: A workshop with a local carver or weaver. Students will handle authentic and replica artifacts to learn the names, meanings, and stories behind specific kōwhaiwhai patterns (e.g., mangōpare - hammerhead shark, symbolizing strength; kape rua - symbolizing new life) used by Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: He Whakaaro Hou: From Pattern to Prototype
activities.description: Using their sketches and new knowledge, students will design a modern whakairo piece. They will create small-scale prototypes using clay or soap, experimenting with how to adapt traditional patterns to tell a new story about their relationship with and responsibility for the awa.

activities.title: Mahi Toi: Creating with Purpose
activities.description: Students will create their final artwork using reclaimed wood or other sustainable materials. This practical session focuses on the application of techniques, material selection, and the intentional communication of their kaitiakitanga message through form and pattern.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga: Exhibition and Korero
activities.description: Students will collaboratively curate a class exhibition in the school whare. They will write artist statements and prepare to present their work and its significance to whānau and community members, explaining the cultural and environmental narratives embedded in their carvings.

resources: Kaumātua and local carvers from Omahu Marae or nearby marae of Ngāti Kahungunu
Digital collection of kōwhaiwhai patterns from Te Papa Tongarewa (online)
Reclaimed timber and carving tools (whao, whakakō) supplied by a local community workshop
The book 'Taonga Tuku Iho: Traditional Māori Art' by Hirini Moko Mead

tasks: A finished whakairo artwork that successfully integrates traditional kōwhaiwhai patterns with a contemporary environmental narrative.
A written or recorded artist statement that uses arts vocabulary to explain the cultural significance of the patterns used and the work's connection to kaitiakitanga of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Active and constructive participation in the collaborative planning and setup of the class exhibition.

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: UC – Research the work of Māori artists and how their art expresses Māori values, such as kaitiakitanga.
Developing Practical Knowledge: PK – Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles for a specific purpose.
Developing Ideas: DI – Generate, develop, and refine visual ideas in response to a variety of motivations, including the study of established practice.
Communicating and Interpreting: CI – Describe and evaluate how ideas and art-making processes are used to communicate meaning in their own and others’ work.
